metadata.heading: bank of india vs. mared shankar rao

page_content: where an accounts clerk of bank used to assist the customers in her bank transactions misrepresented her and received money and it was proved by the signatures on the reverse of the form taken by the bank as an acknowledgement for the receipt of money to be that of the accused he liable to be convicted u/s 467 & 471 ipc.

by machinery, fertilizers, and improved breeds we have increased the yield per acre but nevertheless we are far away from self-sufficiency in production. our population is increasing at a much faster rate than production of food. in the circumstances we have no alternative but to import food grains from outside and regulate our available resources by means of laws. even during years of record harvest in the country, we have imported food grains from outside taxing heavily on our foreign exchange resources. such a step had to be taken to serve as a disincentive to hoarders and profiteers and also for building up a comfortable buffer stock.

the situation is not likely to improve unless the increase in population can be enormously decreased. the stark consequences are that india produces only enough to feed adequately a little less than two-thirds of its population. there is simply not enough food. constantly, unremittingly, the population presses upon the country's resources, increasing every year to consume every increase in resources. we acquire food grains through loans and gifts from rich countries but the rich countries do not generally lend or give unless they can profit by doing so; they expects returns, in the form of strategic involvement, political subservience or other advantages. in this connection we may refer here to the open declaration of donald trump, us president that a country casting a vote against usa on any issue in the united nations organisation (uno) or any other world organisation will lose american aid.

both developing and under-developed country need foreign aid for their progress and prosperity but, unfortunately, rich countries try to exploit the poor nations economic backwardness to achieve their own political interests. international economic relations, whether for monetary behaviour or supply of food, are not without their political components. we must, therefore, not only produce more and import less but at the same time prevent wastage by rats, pestilence etc. at large entertainments more is cooked than is served, more is served than is eaten and more is eaten than is needed. hence the law restricted limited number of guests at parties, and other austerity measure that while controlling consumption, can make possible substantial savings by discipline, sometimes voluntary, often enforced. it must be remembered that together with distribution, supplies and prices also went consumption.

food grain prices constitute the key to the price level as a whole. if the aim is planned economic development with a stable price level, movement of food grain prices calls for the utmost vigilance. as such, undesirable consequences may follow, if the bumper harvest breeds complacency. this means fair and equitable procurement target, strong public distribution system at reasonable prices, in the open market, prevention of wastage and firm legal measures against hoarding and black marketing.

the problem of hoarding and black marketing is comparatively of recent origin. it was during the second world war that many countries including india were forced to resort to rationing. at that time rationing was regarded as a purely, temporary phase. unfortunately, however, partition of the country; failure of crops, year after year, due to natural calamities, such as flood, drought and pestilence; limited industries operating in the country; and population explosion resulted is the shortage of foodstuffs and essential commodities. we have been depending on other countries for supply of foodstuffs and paying heavily for them. unscrupulous businessmen took advantage of the prevailing situation and exploited the public by dictating prices and withholding essential commodities. this led to black marketing.
in order to meet the situation the government found no alternative but to continue rationing and enacted legislation to curb the evil.
the essential commodities act, 1955 empowered the government to regulate the supply of the essential commodities by regulating the trade.

essential commodities have been defined in section 2 of the act. section 3(a) was introduced to include foodstuffs in the list of essential commodities. section 7 of the act provides for penalties up to three years for violation of the provisions of the act. section 8 marks abetment punishable. section 9 provides for punishment for false returns. in addition to this the law of preventive detention was provided for detention of persons whose activities are found to be prejudicial to the essential supplies. notwithstanding the above legislation the people have suffered and continue to suffer at the hands of the black marketers. people, by and large, do not have enough faith in the effectiveness of either the enforcement agencies or the judicial machinery because they find that offenders are either allowed to thrive or they are usually let off with minor punishment or go scot free. consumers are afraid of moving the enforcement agencies because they are apprehensive that essential goods might disappear from the market as soon as any legal action is initiated against the hoarders and the situation might become even more difficult.

consumers are not prepared to complain against the profiteers because under the present law both the buyer and seller are equally liable. this is probably done on the analogy of the anti-corruption laws. both the persons giving and taking bribe are treated as criminals. however, there is basic difference between a buyer in the black market and the person offering a bribe. the latter has a profit motive. the former is just a victim of the circumstances when he buys essential commodities in the black market for consumption. he is not making any profit. on the contrary, he is sacrificing his purchasing power. the high price which he pays is by itself a deterrent against his increasing the demand unnecessarily. his position is essentially different from the seller in the black-market. the seller is working for profit. the economic motive in his case has to be counter-balanced. so it is necessary to threaten him with penal consequences. though the buying and selling constitute one transaction, the natures of the acts are different in the economic context.

this special feature must be taken into consideration. this new approach will be of immense benefit to the enforcement agencies. under the present law, virtually three-fourths of the population is put in the position of criminals.

no law can be effective unless it is backed fully by the force of public conscience. honest citizens who are forced to buy in the black market resent the law which makes them criminals. public conscience is hard against those who exploit the situation for profit, not against those who are victimised by the circumstances. law has to be set in motion by the aggrieved party. by making the possible complainant an accused, we have weakened the enforcement. the seller is well protected against the law because the purchaser, the sole witness and victim has no face to approach the enforcement agencies. furthermore the consumer is not in a position to support his complaint in the absence of any receipt, which is seldom issued and if issued at all, the real price of transaction is never shown. this great handicap will be removed if we treat the consumer differently.

the attitude of the legislature towards the retailers must also change. at present the law makes no difference between a retailer and a wholesale dealer. the retailer has a dual capacity. he is a buyer so for as the wholesaler is concerned. he is a seller so far as the consumer is concerned. he obtains his supplies from the wholesalers on their terms. prices charged by him is dependent on the prices charged by the wholesalers. as long as he takes only reasonable profits and does not hoard, his activities are not antisocial. he dares not complain against wholesalers as in that case the wholesalers may refuse supplies to him and he is thrown out of business. the enforcement agencies prosecute the petty retailers. the wholesalers, who are the real culprits, go scot free. a few such cases force the honest retailers to wind up their business. the enforcement agencies must get at the root and prosecute the wholesalers who control the market; notwithstanding the fact that it is extremely difficult to prosecute the wholesalers because the retailers, who are the only witnesses to prove the transactions, would hesitate to depose against them for fear of starving themselves of their supplies. therefore, retailers rarely have any motive to expose the wholesalers in their own interest.

a clever black marketer, not infrequently, transacts business in the name of a third person. he manipulates records accordingly. the net result is that the big black marketers feel absolutely safe in the courts of law. even if the law prescribes death sentence for black-marketers, the real culprits are least worried about it.

our enforcement agencies and the legal process have failed miserably to bring the real black-marketer, the wholesalers, to book and therefore, notwithstanding prosecution of petty dealers black-marketing is firmly entrenched in our society.

even some of the authorised ration shops indulge in malpractices. it is a routine experience for any ration card-holder to see the midnight oil being burnt by his ration shopkeeper for manipulating accounts and stocks. with rice and wheat of better variety openly available, though at higher prices, many ration card-holders are meeting their requirements from the open market. they even forgo substandard ration quotas consisting of inferior grains mixed with dirt, stones and chaff. this serves the purpose of the authorised ration shopkeepers who make adjustments in the books and sell the stocks not drawn by the card-holders in the black-market.

many corrupt ration shop owners persuade poor card-holders mostly from among illiterate working classes to sell their quotas back to the shop owners after making normal entries in their cards. large quantities of rice, wheat, sugar, maida etc. are thus stored by the ration shop keepers and then sold in the black-market-mainly to the restaurants, who keep on increasing the prices of eatables, especially if the offices establisments happen to be near educational institutions or offices.

hoarders of food grains have learnt by experience not to keep large stocks in their own godowns due to fear that the same may be seized by the government or raided by angry mobs. they purchase farmers produce and leave them in their form-houses. they lift the stock in stages. this does not attract the law.
the problem of maintaining essential supplies is basically an economic problem. in free economy, the factors of demand and supply are adjusted through the price mechanism.

if the supplies are short, the prices rise. in times of scarcity and high prices, the incidence and the extent of adulteration rise, making the food more costlier in terms of money, apart from the detriment it causes to health. this has two-fold effect. the demand is curtailed. high prices also induce more supplies. the equilibrium is thus restored. freedom of trade is essential for the working of adjusting economic forces. most of the countries permit it.

in the sphere of black-marketing and hoarding, the same act may be harmful today, but legitimate tomorrow. it is not possible for the state to take over the trading of all essential commodities and consequently conditions are to be created so that unscrupulous elements in the society do not corner the supplies and force the people to go to the black market.
when the economic forces moral appeals and regulatory measures fail, law is the only remedy.

whoever dishonestly mis-appropriates or converts to his own use any movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

- (a) a takes property belonging to z out of z's possession, in good faith, believing, at any time when he takes it, that the property belongs to himself. a is not guilty of theft; but if a, after discovering his mistake, dishonestly appropriates the property to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section. - (b) a, being on friendly terms with z, goes into z's library in z's absence, and takes away a book without z's express consent. here, if a was under the impression that he had z's implied consent to take the book for the purpose of reading it, a has not committed theft. but, if a afterwards sells the book for his own benefit, he is guilty of an offence under this section. - (c) a and b, being joint owners of a horse, a takes the horse out of b's possession, intending to use it. here, as a has a right to use the horse, he does not dishonestly misappropriate it. but, if a sells the horse and appropriates the whole proceeds to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section.
explanation i.a dishonest misappropriation for a time only is a misappropriation with the meaning of this section.

a finds a government promissory note belonging to z, bearing a blank endorsement. a, knowing that the note belongs to z, pledges it with a banker as a security for a loan, intending at a future time to restore it to z. a has committed an offence under this section.

explanation 2.a person who finds property not in the possession of any other person, and takes such property for the purpose of protecting if for, or of restoring it to, the owner does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly, and is not guilty of an offence; but he is guilty of the offence above defined, if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner, or before he has used reasonable means to discover and give notice to the owner and has kept the property a reasonable time to enable the owner to claim it.
what are reasonable means or what is a reasonable time in such a case, is a question of fact.

it is not necessary that the finder should know who is the owner of the property, or that any particular person is the owner of it; it is sufficient if, at the time of appropriating it, he does not believe it to be his own property, or in good faith believe that the real owner cannot be found.

- (a) a finds a rupee on the high road, not knowing to whom the rupee belongs. a picks up the rupee. here a has not committed the offence defined in this section. - (b) a finds a letter on the road, containing a bank note. from the direction and contents of the letter he learns to whom the note belongs. he appropriates the note. he is guilty of an offence under this section. - (c) a finds a cheque payable to bearer. he can form no conjecture as to the person who has lost the cheque. but the name of the person, who has drawn the cheque, appears. a knows that this person can direct him to the person in whose favour the cheque was drawn. a appropriates the cheque without attempting to discover the owner. he is guilty of an offence under this section. - (d) a sees z drop his purse with money in it. a picks up the purse with the intention of restoring it to z, but afterwards appropriates it to his own use. a has committed an offence under this section. - (e) a finds a purse with money, not knowing to whom it belongs; he afterwards discovers that it belongs to z, and appropriates it to his own use. a is guilty of an offence under this section. - (f) a finds a valuable ring, not knowing to whom it belongs. a sells it immediately without attempting to discover the owner. a is guilty of an offence under this section.

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: dishonest misappropriation of property possessed by deceased person at the time of his death.-

page_content: whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use property, knowing that such property was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of that person's decease, and has not since been in the possession of any person legally entitled to such possession, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offender at the time of such person's decease was employed by him as a clerk or servant, the imprisonment may extend to seven years.

z dies in possession of furniture and money. his servant a, before the money comes into the possession of any person entitled to such possession, dishonestly misappropriates it. a has committed the offence defined in this section.

sec. 405 ipc/ 314 bns: 'whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates & converts to his own use that property or dishonestly uses or dispossess of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged or of any legal contract express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or willfully suffers any other person so to do, commits criminal breach, trust'.

to constitute the offence of criminal breach of trust, there must be dishonest misappropriation by a person in whom confidence is placed as to the custody & management of the property in respect of which the breach of trust is charges.

there must be an entrustment; there must be misappropriation or conversion to one's own use or use in violation any legal direction or of any legal contract; and thirdly the misappropriation or conversion or disposal must be with dishonest intention.

the expression 'entrustment' carries with it, the implication that the person handing over any property or on whose behalf that property is handed over to another, continuous to be its owner. the person handing over the property must have confidence in the person taking the property so as to create a fiduciary relationship between them.

a trust is on obligation annexed to the ownerships of property and arising out of a confidence reposed in and accepted by the owner and declared and accepted by him, for the benefit of another and the owner.
the property regarding which the offence is alleged to have committed must have been entrusted to the accused and he must have dominion over it.

a wife has a joint possession of her husband's property and cannot therefore, be indicated to disposing of it in any way. where the exclusive property of one of the spouses is unwarrantedly taken away or misappropriated by the other and the marriage breaks down, the offence of misappropriation can be made out. if the stridhana property of a married woman is placed in the custody of her husband or in laws, they would be deemed to be trustees and bound to return the same, if and when demanded by her. (1985 cr.l.j. 819).
pledge:a person who pledges what is pledged to him in violation of any direction of law or contract or denies pledge, is quality of this offence.

hire purchases agreement: if a person gets an article under hire purchase agreement and subsequently sells it, without making payment in full to the person from whom he has hired it, he commits criminal breach trust.

mere retention of property entrusted to a person without any misappropriation is not criminal breach of trust. unless there is some actual user by the accused which is in violation of law or contract, there is no criminal breach of trust and even if there is such user, there must be dishonest intention.

the misappropriation, conversion etc., must be violation of a legal direction prescribing the mode in which the person entrusted with the property is to carry out the trust or contract. aggravated forms of criminal breach of trust is u/s 407 to 409 ipc.
criminal breach trust committed by carrier what finger or, warehouse keeper is punishable u/s 407 of ipc.
criminal breach of trust committed by a clerk, or servant or employed as a clerk or servant is punishable u/s. 408 of ipc.
criminal breach of trust committed by banker, merchant factor, broker, attorney or agent is punishable u/s 409 of ipc.

the following ipc/ bns provisions are commonly invoked in the investigation of economic offences:-
it is important for an investigating officer to understand the ingredients of each of these offences. but prior to that, meaning of the following terns would need to be understood clearly.
the terms wrongful gain and wrongful loss are defined in section 23 of ipc 2(36), 2(38) bns wrongful gain is any gain made by unlawful means to which the accused person is not legally entitled.
the term dishonestly defined in section 24 ipc/ 2(7) bns.
it means
- i. doing a thing - ii. with intent to cause - iii. wrongful gain or wrongful loss.

the term fraudulently is defined in section 25 of 1pc/ 2(9) bns. it means doing a thing with the intention to commit fraud. as the term fraud is not defined in ipc, its definition contained in section 17, indian contract act will apply as follows: 'fraud' means and includes any of the following acts committed by a party to a contract, or with his connivance, or by his agent, with intent to deceive another party thereto or his agent, or to induce him to enter into the contract:

- i. the suggestion, as a fact, of that which is not true, by one who does not believe it to be true; - ii. the active concealment of a fact by one having knowledge or belief of the fact; - iii. a promise made without an intention of performing it;
- iv. any other act fitted to deceive; - v. any such act or omission as the law specially declares to be fraudulent.
the term valuable security is defined in section 30 ipc as any document whereby any legal right is created, transferred, restricted, extinguished or released.
cheating is defined as

- i. an act of fraudulently or dishonestly inducing the victim to deliver any property to any person (which he would not have delivered otherwise), or - ii. intentionally inducing the victim to do or omit to do something which he would not have done otherwise, and which act or omission causes or likely to cause damage or harm to the victim in body, mind, reputation or property.
explanation:dishonestly concealing facts amounts to deception.
simple cheating is an offence under section 417 ipc and is punishable only up to one year or fine or both. this offence is bailable and non-cognizable.

this section defines the offence of cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property. in other words, cheating alone does not constitute the offence of 420 ipc. the acts of omission and commission on the part of the accused should also dishonestly induce the victim to part with some property or make, alter or destroy any valuable security. this offence is punishable with imprisonment of up to seven years and fine, both.
whoever cheats a person and thereby dishonestly induces him or her to

- i. deliver any property to any person, or - ii. make, alter or destroy any valuable security, or anything which is capable of being converted into valuable security, commits the offence of 420 ipc/ 318 bns.
making a false document in defined in section 464 ipc/ 335 bns. ingredients a person is said to make a false document if:-

- i. he dishonestly or fraudulently makes, signs, seals or executes a document or part of a document with the intention of causing it to be believed that such document or part of document has been made signed, sealed or executive under the authority of a person who has not actually made, signed, sealed or executed that document, or - ii. he dishonestly or fraudulently and without legal authority alters a document after it has already been made, executed or signed by himself or by any other person or, - iii. he dishonestly or fraudulently induces any other person to sign, seal, execute or alter a document knowing full well that such person does not know the content of the document nature of the alternation because of - i. unsoundness of mind - ii. intoxication, or - iii. deception practiced by the accused.
the term forgery is defined in section 463 ipc/ 336 bns.
ingredients

- i. whoever makes a false document, - ii. with intention to cause damage or injury to the public or any person, or - iii. to support any claim or title, or - iv. to cause any person to part with property, or - v. to enter into any contract, or - vi. with intent to commit fraud, - vii. commits the offence of forgery.
this section provides punishment for simple forgery - imprisonment up to two years or fine, or both. this offence is non-cognizable and bailable.

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: c) section 467 ipc (forgery of a valuable security)/ 338 bns

page_content: this section prescribes punishment for forgery of valuable security. both these terms forgery and valuable security have been explained above. maximum punishment is life term. in spite of being punishable with life sentence or imprisonment for 10 years and fine, this offence is noncognizable, non-cognizable and triable by magistrateof 1st class. only if the forgery is of a promissory note of the central government, the offence is cognizable.

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: d) section 468 ipc (forgery for the purpose of cheating)/ 338 bns

page_content: 468 1pc defines the offence of forgery for the purpose of cheating. maximum punishment is seven years. here, forgery may be simple or of a valuable security. this offence is cognizable and non-bailable.

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: e) section 471 ipc (using a forged document as genuine)/ 340 bns

page_content: if a person fraudulently or dishonestly uses a forged document as genuine, which he knows or has reason to believe to be forged, he commits the offence of 471 ipc/ 340 bns. this offence is cognizable and bailable.
offence of criminal conspiracy requires minimum two persons.
ingredients when two or more persons agree to do or cause to be done
- i. illegal act, or - ii. a legal act by illegal means, such an agreement is termed as criminal conspiracy.

sec 2 (b) 'chit' means a transaction whether called chit, chit fund, chitty, kuri or by any other name by or under which a person enters into an agreement with a specified number of persons that everyone of them shall subscribe a certain sum of money (or a certain quantity of grain instead) by way of periodical installments over a definite period and that each such subscriber shall in his turn as determined by lot or by auction or by tender or in such other manner as may be specified in the chit agreement, be entitled to the prize amount.
sec. 2 (c) chit agreement- means the document contraining the articles of agreement between the foreman and the subscribers relating to the chit;
sec. 2 (d) - chit amount means the sum-total of the subscriptions payable by all the subscribers for any installment of a chit without any deductions of amount or otherwise.

sec. 2 (g) - 'discount' means the sum of money or the quatity of grain which a prized subscriber is, under the terms of the chit agreement, required to forego and which is set apart under the said agreement to meet the expenses of running the chit or for distribution among the subscribers or for both: at each installment of chit
sec. 2 (h) - 'dividend' means the share of the subscriber in the amount of discount available under the chit agreement for retable distribution among the subscribers.
sec. 2 (j) - 'foreman' means the person who under the chit agreement is responsible for the conduct of the chit and includes any person discharging the junctions of the foreman u/s. 3g.
sec. 2 (m) - 'prize amount' means the difference between the chit amount and the discount.
sec. 4 - prohibition of chits not sanctioned or registered under the act.
sec. 5 - prohibition of invitation for subscription without the previous sanction required u/s. 4 of the act.
sec. 76 - punishment: 2 years or wit fine which may extend to five thousands rupees or with both.

(i) all offences u/s., 11 shall be cognizable.
sec. 80 (2): no court inferior to that of a metropolitan magistrate or a judicial magistrate of the first class shall try an offence punishable under this act.

is an act of the parliament of india enacted by the nda government to prevent money-laundering and to provide for confiscation of property derived from money-laundering. pmla and the rules notified there under came into force with effect from july 1, 2005. the act and rules notified there under impose obligation on banking companies, financial institutions and intermediaries to verify identity of clients, maintain records and furnish information in prescribed form to financial intelligence unit - india (fiuind).
the act was amended in the year 2005, 2009 and 2012.

on 24 nov 2017, in a ruling in favour of citizens' liberty, the supreme court has set aside a clause in the prevention of money laundering act, which made it virtually impossible for a person convicted to more than three years in jail to get bail if the public prosecutor opposed it. (section 45 of the pmla act, 2002, provides that no person can be granted bail for any offence under the act unless the public prosecutor, appointed by the government, gets a chance to oppose his bail. and should the public prosecutor choose to oppose bail, the court has to be convinced that the accused was not guilty of the crime and additionally that he/she was not likely to commit any offence while out on bail- a tall order by any count.) (it observed that the provision violates articles 14 and 21 of the indian constitution)
the pmla seeks to combat money laundering in india and has three main objectives:
- to prevent and control money laundering - to confiscate and seize the property obtained from the laundered money; and - to deal with any other issue connected with money laundering in india.

- attachment: prohibition of transfer, conversion, disposition or movement of property by an appropriate legal order. - proceeds of crime: any property derived or obtained, directly or indirectly, by any person as a result of criminal activity relating to a scheduled offence. - money-laundering: whosoever directly or indirectly attempts to indulge or assist other person or actually involved in any activity connected with the proceeds of crime and projecting it as untainted property.

- payment system: a system that enables payment to be effected between a payer and a beneficiary, involving clearing, payment or settlement service or all of them. it includes the systems enabling credit card, debit card, smart card, money transfer or similar operations.
punishment for money-laundering

- the act prescribes that any person found guilty of money-laundering shall be punishable with rigorous imprisonment from three years to seven years and where the proceeds of crime involved relate to any offence under paragraph 2 of part a of the schedule (offences under the narcotic drugs and psychotropic substance act, 1985), the maximum punishment may extend to 10 years instead of 7 years.

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: powers of attachment of tainted property

page_content: - appropriate authorities, appointed by the govt of india, can provisionally attach property believed to be "proceeds of crime" for 180 days. such an order is required to be confirmed by an independent adjudicating authority.

- the adjudicating authority is the authority appointed by the central government through notification to exercise jurisdiction, powers and authority conferred under pmla. it decides whether any of the property attached or seized is involved in money laundering. - the adjudicating authority shall not be bound by the procedure laid down by the code of civil procedure,1908, but shall be guided by the principles of natural justice and subject to the other provisions of pmla. the adjudicating authority shall have powers to regulate its own procedure.

- where money laundering involves two or more inter-connected transactions and one or more such transactions is or are proved to be involved in money laundering, then for the purposes of adjudication or confiscation, it shall presumed that the remaining transactions form part of such inter-connected transactions.
- a person, who is accused of having committed the offence of money laundering, has to prove that alleged proceeds of crime are in fact lawful property.

- an appellate tribunal is the body appointed by govt of india. it is given the power to hear appeals against the orders of the adjudicating authority and any other authority under the act.orders of the tribunal can be appealed in appropriate high court (for that jurisdiction) and finally to the supreme court

- section 43 of prevention of money laundering act, 2002 (pmla) says that the central government, in consultation with the chief justice of the high court, shall, for trial of offence punishable under section 4, by notification, designate one or more courts of session as special court or special courts for such area or areas or for such case or class or group of cases as may be specified in the notification.

- financial intelligence unit -india (fiu-ind) was set by the government of india on 18 november 2004 as the central national agency responsible for receiving, processing, analyzing and disseminating information relating to suspect financial transactions. fiu-ind is also responsible for coordinating and strengthening efforts of national and international intelligence, investigation and enforcement agencies in pursuing the global efforts against money laundering and related crimes. fiu-ind is an independent body reporting directly to the economic intelligence council (eic) headed by the finance minister.

metadata.heading: history and types of organized crime

page_content: the crimes, which are organized, are real dangers to the society. the criminals act in a organized manner and gain economical benefit and also political advantage. the organized crimes are of two kinds..

page_content: 1. predatory crimes. 2. racketeering crimes

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: history and types of organized crime

page_content: predatory crimes are those which are committed by two or more persons cooperatively for their own purpose for gain. the robbery, dacoity, theft, cheating, burglary, counterfeiting of coins etc... are such offences. the people are victim of these offences. the crimes are committed in gangs. robbery and dacoity belongs to category of violent character.

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: history and types of organized crime

page_content: racketeering is rendering in organized manner illicit goods or services to the consumers who generally do legitimate activities. providing services or goods may not appear to be harmful but really speaking it may have far reaching consequences on the society.

metadata.subject: special_crimes
metadata.heading: history and types of organized crime

page_content: trafficking of arms, human beings (prostitution, slavery, begging), wildlife, gambling, illicit liquor, smuggling of narcotic drugs, cattle, antiques, hawaala, dealing pornography etc... these are in operation in the society in organized fashion having administrative and political links racketeering has the patronage of bureaucracy and political leaders. the politicians for their survival depend on the local gangs.

1. in the materialistic advancement of society, people want status and for the status, they desire to have material things. 2. to satisfy these desires in lawful manner is not possible so they indulge in racketeering and commit organized crimes. 3. in many types of racketeering, compulsion is an important factor which leads to violence sometimes. the people on the basis of muscle power and wide network become successful in taking contracts by forming syndicates, kidnapping and abduction for ransom. 4. the criminals have established their network throughout the country and at international level.

the international criminal police organisation, (interpol) defines organised criminals as any individuals or group of persons engaged in continuing illegal activity which has as its primary purpose, the generation of profits, irrespective of national boundaries. according to the 'omnibus crime control & safe streets act' enacted by the us congress in the late sixties, 'organised crime includes the unlawful activities of the members of a highly organised, disciplined association engaged in supplying illegal goods and services, including but not limited to gambling, prostitution, loan sharking, narcotics, labour racketeering and other unlawful activities of such associations'.
based on the distinctive underlying features, organised criminals can be classified into three categories:
1. gangsters 2. racketeers 3. syndicates
gangster : a member of gang or group of violent criminals.
racketer : a person, who engages in dishonest and fraudulent business dealings.
syndicate : a group of individuals or organizations combined to promote a common interest, a committee of syndics.
organised gangs comprise of hardened criminals who are not new to the world of crime and criminality. they did exist even during pre-independence days.

' organised crime ' means any continuing unlawful activity by an individual, singly or jointly, either as a member of an organised crime syndicate or on behalf of such syndicate, by use of violence or threat of violence or intimidation or coercion, or other unlawful means, with the objective of gaining pecuniary benefits, or gaining undue economic or other advantage for himself or any other person or promoting insurgency. ' organised crime syndicate ' means a group of two or more persons who, acting either singly or collectively, as a syndicate or gang indulge in activities of organised crime.
in the united states, the organized crime control act (1970) defines organized crime as 'the unlawful activities of a highly organized, disciplined association'.

european sociologist diego gambetta defines the mafia as a type of organized crime group that specializes in the supply of extra-legal protection and quasi law enforcement. organized crime is a category of transnational, national, or local groupings of highly centralized enterprises run by criminals who intend to engage in illegal activity, most commonly for money and profit.

organized crime is defined as the product of a self perpetuating criminal conspiracy to bring exorbitant profits from society by any means fair, foul, legal and illegal. the two important elements of this form of collective deviance are the self perpetuating nature and the criminal intent.

it is a matter of common knowledge that all activities which are not impulsive have to be organised although the element of organization may differ widely depending upon the nature of activity. for example, an industrial unit is an organization in the sense that it has to secure the raw material for its purpose, go through different stages of manufacture, assemble the various parts into a final product and market them for the general public. for all these sub activities the system needs man power, resources and leadership. the same pattern exists in respect of collective criminal activity which may be called organised crime. it is organized in the sense that it is a set-up in which duties and functions are divided and there is a hierarchy of leadership to oversee its activity, derive profits and distribute them to the members.

an element of confusion exists in regard to the term organized crime, because, it is not strictly an individual traditional crime but a collective activity in respect of certain types of anti social behaviour for which there is some amount of general public demand.

organized crime is 'self-perpetuating ' because if one leader is removed, another takes his place just as in any normal organization. the conspiracy- part emerges from the fact that certain norms, conditions and terms are accepted by all members when they join the organization. in short, organized crime has also a high degree of unity by the very nature of its activities which do not come to the surface easily and even if they do, they are spread over in such a manner that suppression is very difficult. organized crime came to the forefront in the early thirties in the usa where on account of urbanization, cities grew in a hap hazard manner and many criminal or quasi criminal activities were monopolized by certain criminal elements who joined together.

these activities related to gambling, prostitution, loans at exorbitant rates, taking protection money, sale of alcoholic liquor and entertainment. some of the organizations started as bonafide business ventures known as holding companies which obtained control of a number of business interests through buying out stocks, adopting illegal channels of supply etc which is black- marketing. the object of such companies was not to produce anything tangible but merely to extract money from the investors. this is called 'racketeering' and the term racket has come to be often used in discussing organized crime.

how organized crime grew in some of the american cities in the thirties. a very notorious racketeer of those times was a1 capone who was an immigrant from italy and built up a gigantic criminal enterprise monopolising the entire bootlegging business during the prohibition days. it was estimated that at the height of his power he controlled bootlegging in four states and his gross income from illicit liquor was as much 6 million dollars in a week. the main point is that this gangster could never have been able to command such power unless there were also thousands of respectable people who were willing to evade the law by purchasing or retailing illicit liquor. it is the essence of organized crime that whatever may be the field of operation, some amount of public support is forthcoming. it applies equally to gambling, prostitution, drug trafficking shady monetary deals etc.

it is also in the nature of things that if one person becomes powerful through a particular method or activity, many other competitors also emerge. this happens invariably in all organized crime activities. the competitors vie with each other for having control of such activities in a particular area. when their interests clash, there is intense rivalry between the groups or crime gangs for leadership. attempts are made by both to eliminate the others through violence. violence in organized crime is therefore the end product of competition in criminal activity.

two major features of organized crime are noteworthy. the first is that organized crime is essentially a part of the urban development process and is therefore more predominant in big and growing cities and, to a limited extent in big towns.

it consists of brutal murders and elimination of rivals in illegal activities like smuggling, bootlegging, and prostitution. the second feature relates to element of collusion of the police and other enforcement agencies with organized crime gangs.

bootlegging, prostitution, gambling, manipulation of bids or tenders in auctions and contracts, land grabbing, illegal possession or dispossession of property, protection money, rigging elections, loan sharking, extortion, kidnapping for ransom, drug trafficking, illicit trade in fire arms, explosives, smuggling, thefts of antiquities and cultural properties, trading in animal skins and human organs are some of the activities of criminal groups, which are sometimes small outfits and sometimes large. the area of operation of these groups may extend to districts or states or even countries. there are groups bound to an individual or to each other. generally there is always a leader or head of such groups.

'drug trafficking' is a global illicit trade involving the cultivation, manufacture, distribution and sale of substances which are subject to drug prohibition laws. unodc is continuously monitoring and researching global illicit drug markets in order to gain a more comprehensive understanding of their dynamics. drug trafficking is a key part of the research.

at current levels, world heroin consumption (340 tons) and seizures represent an annual flow of 430-450 tons of heroin into the global heroin market. of that total, opium from myanmar and the lao people's democratic republic yields some 50 tons, while the rest, some 380 tons of heroin and morphine, is produced exclusively from afghanistan opium. while approximately 5 tons are consumed and seized in afghanistan, the remaining bulk of 375 tons is trafficked worldwide via routes flowing into and through the countries neighboring afghanistan.

the balkan and northern routes are the main heroin trafficking corridors linking afghanistan to the huge markets of the russian federation and western europe. the balkan route traverses the islamic republic of iran (often via pakistan), turkey, greece and bulgaria across south-east europe to the western european market, with an annual market value of some $20 billion. the northern route runs mainly through tajikistan and kyrgyzstan (or uzbekistan or turkmenistan) to kazakhstan and the russian federation. the size of that market is estimated to total $13 billion per year.

during the last few decades, indian enforcement agencies have seized heroin and other opiate drugs worth crores of rupees in the international market, the contraband having been smuggled from afghanistan via pakistan across the punjab borders. these seizures which have received wide publicity merely represent the tip of an iceberg. the extent of smuggling and clandestine manufacture of drugs as well as drug addiction in the country is anybody's guess. the union minister of state for social welfare announced some time ago that fresh surveys would be carried out to assess the magnitude of drug addiction and the present law covers not merely the smuggling aspect but also envisages steps to curb the growing addiction to drugs particularly among the youth.

drugs are of different kinds. they are opiates like (opium, heroin, morphine). hallucinogens (marijuana, hashish), stimulants (cocaine, amphetamines), barbiturates and psychodelic substances like lsd (lysergic acid diethylamide), which lead to strong hallucinations. drug addiction is self destructive and hence society has a responsibility to control it. more importantly, some drugs by injection contribute to aggressive attitudes and the craving for them drives people to commit crime.
thus both from the points of view of health and prevention of crime, drug traffic has reached frightening proportions and poses a serious challenge to law enforcement agencies.

most of the opiates are derived from indian hemp ( cannabis ), a plant that produces psychoactive resin in its flowering tops. the resin which is generally called charas or hashish has been known to be in use in india from time immemorial. many of the derivatives from the plants are used through chemical processes to produce deleterious drugs whose addiction has become world-wide. lsd was virtually unknown till the hippie culture came into vogue in the sixties. lsd became a profitable addition to the black marketers dealing in drugs.

morphine and heroin are the most well known opiate drugs which are associated with painkilling. less known, but medically used are codeine, me piridine and methadone . these drugs are generally depressant and their effects are relief of pain, sedation, muscular relaxation, hypnosis and a sense - of well being.
drug trafficking is a global illicit trade involving the cultivation. manufacture, distribution, and sale of substances which are subject to drug prohibition laws.
unodc (united nations office on drug control) is continuously monitoring and researching global illicit drug markets to gain a more comprehensive understanding of their dynamics.

the crime investigation department (cid) is the investigation wing of the indian state police.

the cid was created by the british government in 1902, based on the recommendations of the police commission. at the entrance of the cid office at gokhale marg, lucknow, there is a portrait of rai bahadur pandit shambhu nath, king's police medalist (kpm) and member of british empire (mbe) with a caption "father of indian cid". in 1929, the cid was split into special branch, cid and the crime branch (cb-cid).
the cid has several branches which differ from state to state. these branches include:
- anti-human trafficking - fake indian currency notes (ficn) - missing persons cell - anti-narcotics cell - finger print bureau - general offences wing - economic offences wing

cb-cid is a special wing in the cid headed by the additional director general of police (adgp) and assisted by the inspector general of police (igp). this branch investigates serious crimes including riots, forgery, counterfeiting and cases entrusted to cb-cid by the state government or the high court.

the directorate of enforcement is a law enforcement agency and economic intelligence agency responsible for enforcing economic laws and fighting economic crime in india.[1] it is part of the department of revenue, ministry of finance, government of india. it is composed of officers from the indian revenue service, indian corporate law service, indian police service and the indian administrative service. the origin of this directorate goes back to 1 may 1956, when an 'enforcement unit' was formed, in department of economic affairs, for handling exchange control laws violations under foreign exchange regulation act, 1947. in the year 1957, this unit was renamed as 'enforcement directorate'.

the prime objective of the enforcement directorate is the enforcement of two key acts[4] of the government of india namely, the foreign exchange management act 1999 (fema) and the prevention of money laundering act 2002 (pmla)
the ed's objective is primarily checking money laundering in india.

the directorate of enforcement, with its headquarters at new delhi is headed by the director of enforcement. there are five regional offices at mumbai, chennai, chandigarh, kolkata and delhi headed by special directors of enforcement.

zonal offices of the directorate are at ahmedabad, bangalore, chandigarh, chennai, kochi, delhi, panaji, guwahati, hyderabad, jaipur, jalandhar, kolkata, lucknow, mumbai, patna and srinagar. these are headed by a joint director.

the directorate has sub-zonal offices at bhubaneshwar, kozhikode, indore, madurai, nagpur, prayagraj, raipur, dehradun, ranchi, surat, shimla , jammu and visakhapatnam which are headed by a deputy director.

for the trial of an offence punishable under section 4 of pmla, the central government (in consultation with the chief justice of the high court), designates one or more sessions court as special court(s). the court is also called "pmla court". any appeal against any order passed by pmla court can directly be filed in the high court for that jurisdiction.

functions with the central board of excise and customs (cbec), in the ministry of finance, department of revenue. headed by director general in new delhi, it is presently divided into seven zones, each under the charge of an additional director general, and further sub-divided into regions of additional directors, joint directors, deputy directors, assistant directors, senior intelligence officers and intelligence officers. its charter includes the following functions:

- collection of intelligence about smuggling of contraband goods, narcotics, under-invoicing etc. through sources of india and abroad, including secret sources. - analysis and dissemination of such intelligence to the field formations for action. - working out of intelligence by the directorate officers themselves to a successful conclusion, where necessary. - keeping watch over important seizures and investigation of cases. - associating or taking over the investigations which warrant specialized handling by the directorate. - guiding important investigation/ prosecution cases. - functioning as the liaison authority for exchange of information among escap countries for combating international smuggling and customs frauds in terms of the recommendations of the escap conference. - keeping liaison with foreign countries, indian missions and enforcement agencies abroad on anti-smuggling matters. - to keep liaison with central bureau of investigation and through them with the interpol. - to coordinate, direct and control anti-smuggling operations on the indo-nepal border. - to refer cases registered under the customs act to the income tax department for action under the income tax act. - to keep statistics of seizures and prices/ rates etc. for watching trends of smuggling and supply required material to the ministry of finance and other ministries. - to study and suggest remedies for loopholes in law and procedures to combat smuggling.

the income tax department , also referred to as it department , is a government agency in charge of monitoring the income tax collection by the government of india. it functions under the department of revenue of the ministry of finance. it is responsible for administering following direct taxation acts passed by parliament of india.
- the income-tax act, 1961 - expenditure tax act, 1987 - various finance acts (passed every year in budget session)

the it department is also responsible for enforcing the double taxation avoidance agreements and deals with various aspects of international taxation such as transfer pricing. finance act, 2012 seeks to grant income tax department powers to combat aggressive tax avoidance by enforcing general anti avoidance rules.

the central board of direct taxes (cbdt) is a part of department of revenue in the ministry of finance. the cbdt provides inputs for policy and planning of direct taxes in india, and is also responsible for administration of direct tax laws through the it department. the cbdt is a statutory authority functioning under the central board of revenue act, 1963. the officials of the board in their ex officio capacity also function as a division of the ministry dealing with matters relating to levy and collection of direct taxes. the cbdt is headed by chairman and also comprises six members, all of whom are ex officio special secretary to the government of india.

the chairman and members of the cbdt are selected from the indian revenue service (irs), whose members constitute the top management of the it department. the chairman and every member of cbdt are responsible for exercising supervisory control over definite areas of field offices of it department, known as zones. various functions and responsibilities of the cbdt are distributed amongst chairman and six members, with only fundamental issues reserved for collective decision by the cbdt.

the areas for collective decision by the cbdt include policy regarding discharge of statutory functions of the cbdt and of the union government under the various direct tax laws. they also include general policy relating to:

- set up and structure of income tax department; - methods and procedures of work of the cbdt; - measures for disposal of assessments, collection of taxes, prevention and detection of tax evasion and tax avoidance;

- recruitment, training and all other matters relating to service conditions and career prospects of all personnel of the income-tax department; - laying down of targets and fixing of priorities for disposal of assessments and collection of taxes and other related matters; - write off of tax demand exceeding rs.25 lakhs in each case; - policy regarding grant of rewards and appreciation certificates.

any other matter, which the chairman or any member of the board, with the approval of the chairman, may refer for joint consideration of the board. the powers of tax recovery officers (tros) were increased by government of india in june 2016.
the finance ministry instructed all revenue intelligence agencies to join the crackdown on forex traders, hawala operators and jewellers besides tracking movement of demonetised currency notes.

income tax departments raided various illegal tax-evasive businesses in delhi, mumbai, chandigarh, ludhiana and other cities that traded with demonetised currency. the enforcement directorate issued several fema notices to forex and gold traders. large sum of cash in defunct notes were seized in different parts of the country.

financial intelligence unit is the gathering of information about the financial affairs of entities of interest, to understand their nature and capabilities, and predict their intentions. generally the term applies in the context of law enforcement and related activities.

one of the main purposes of financial intelligence is to identify financial transactions that may involve tax evasion, money laundering or some other criminal activity. finint may also be involved in identifying financing of criminal and terrorist organisations.
